title: C Programming - A Historical Perspective - www.cc4e.com
description:
The https://www.cc4e.com web site is dedicated to learning the "classic" version of
the C Programming language from the 1978 book written by Brian W. Kernighan and Dennis M. Ritchie.

This K&R book places the reader in the middle of the 1970's transition from a hardware-centered computer science to a focus on writing portable and efficient software.  C was used to develop operating systems like Unix, Minix, and Linux.

This course is part of an online programming curriculum at https://online.dr-chuck.com/

title: A History of C, UNIX, and Computation (a.k.a "The importance of 1978")
description:
In this lecture we cover how so many trends in computer hardware, operating systems, programming languages, and the overall approach to Computer Science were in flux and vastly influences by the C programming language which was introduced to the world in 1978.

For more details and a set of exercises please visit https://www.cc4e.com
For the full curriculum, see https://online.dr-chuck.com/

title: C Programming - Chapter 00 - Introduction
description:
The preface to C Programming by Brian W. Kernighan and Dennis M. Ritchie places the C programming language in the context of the other popular programming languages of the 1960's and 1970's FORTRAN, COBOL, Pascal, Algol, and PL/I .  Many concepts like separation of concerns and the use of provided run-time libraries versus language syntax are introduced and described.  

For more detail see https://www.cc4e.com.

This material is used under "fair use" so we can interpret this historical and scholarly work and place it in the context of the history of computing.

title: From Python to C - The Rosetta Stone Lecture
description:
In this lecture we work through a series of equivalent examples between Python and C as a way to get a broad understanding of C before diving into the details of the language.

For more details and a set of exercises please visit https://www.cc4e.com
For the full curriculum, see https://online.dr-chuck.com/

Dr. Chuck's Article on Object Oriented Languages: https://www.quora.com/Is-C-object-oriented-or-procedural/answer/Chuck-Severance

title: Understanding Chapter 6 of K&R C (1978 Edition)
description:
In this lecture we provide some context for Chapter 6 (Structures) of the Kernighan and Ritchie 1978 edition of C Programming.  In particular, the chapter makes a quick transition from explaining the 'structure' keyword and how it is used to covering *data* structures like linked lists, trees, and hash maps.  There is a lot of information in the 21 pages that make up Chapter 6.  This lecture features reading of a poem by Robert Frost that inform how Dr. Chuck views the material in Chapter 6 (miles to go before we sleep :) ).

For more details and a set of exercises please visit https://www.cc4e.com
For the full curriculum, see https://online.dr-chuck.com/

title: Object Oriented Programming - A Historical Perspective
description:
In this lecture we look at how the Object Oriented Programming (OOP) was implemented in programming languages from 1960 to the present.  We compare how languages evolved and see how the OOP patterns in those languages evolved in parallel. 

For more details and a set of exercises please visit https://www.cc4e.com
For the full curriculum, see https://online.dr-chuck.com/

title: Comparing Object Oriented Programming Approaches Across Languages
description:
In this lecture we compare and contrast how OOP is implemented in C++, Python, Java, JavaScript, PHP, and C#.  We look at how each language syntax can be used to define and use objects.

For more details and a set of exercises please visit https://www.cc4e.com
For the full curriculum, see https://online.dr-chuck.com/

title: Implementing Python-like Object Oriented Patterns in C
description:
In this lecture we look at how we might support Object Oriented Programming in a language like C using structures, pointers, and dynamic memory.  We look at interfaces, naming conventions, and implementation patterns.  In a way we are looking at how Python implemented its Object Oriented support in C.

For more details and a set of exercises please visit https://www.cc4e.com
For the full curriculum, see https://online.dr-chuck.com/

title: Implementing the Python String (str) Class in C
description:
In this lecture, we build structures and code to implement an approximation of the Python str() class in C.

For more details and a set of exercises please visit https://www.cc4e.com
For the full curriculum, see https://online.dr-chuck.com/

title: Implementing the Python list() Class in C
description:
In this lecture, we build structures and code to implement an approximation of the Python list() class in C.

For more details and a set of exercises please visit https://www.cc4e.com
For the full curriculum, see https://online.dr-chuck.com/

description:

For the full curriculum, see https://online.dr-chuck.com/

title: Implementing Encapsulation and Interfaces in C (01)
description:
In this lecture, we look at how an object oriented framework would implement encapsulation using C structures.

For more details and a set of exercises please visit https://www.cc4e.com
For the full curriculum, see https://online.dr-chuck.com/

title: Exploring the Map Abstraction across Languages (02)
description:
In this lecture we look at the concept of maps in Python, PHP, C++, and Java in order to better understand abstraction, interfaces, and the separation of interface and implementation.

For more details and a set of exercises please visit https://www.cc4e.com
For the full curriculum, see https://online.dr-chuck.com/

Dr. Chuck's Hoodie in this lecture is from the Electronic Freedom Foundation which you get by sending in a donation at https://supporters.eff.org/donate

title: A Quick Look at Unique Aspects of the C++ Object Oriented Pattern (03)
description:
In this lecture we look at how C++ implements operator overloading which leads to elegant syntax structures which were adopted by Python in its design of Object Orientation.  We also review call by reference in C++ at how Python implemented square bracket syntax without using call by reference.  We also look at how these use cases are mapped onto Java which neither has fancy syntax not call by references - but instead relies on a setter / getter pattern.

For more details and a set of exercises please visit https://www.cc4e.com
For the full curriculum, see https://online.dr-chuck.com/

Dr. Chuck's Hoodie in this lecture is from the Electronic Freedom Foundation which you can get from https://supporters.eff.org/donate

title: Implementing Encapsulation in our C-Based Objects (04)
description:
In this lecture we start to build our refactored MapList implementation.   Our first step is to use pointers to functions in our Map structure instead of global unctions with naming conventions.  Storing the attributes and metohods "together" is an important aspect of encapsulation.

For more details and a set of exercises please visit https://www.cc4e.com
For the full curriculum, see https://online.dr-chuck.com/

Dr. Chuck's Hoodie in this lecture is from the Electronic Freedom Foundation which you can get from https://supporters.eff.org/donate

title: Building an Iterator abstraction in C (05)
description:
In this lecture we look at how to build an iterator for our MapList implementation.   

For more details and a set of exercises please visit https://www.cc4e.com
For the full curriculum, see https://online.dr-chuck.com/

title: Introduction: Building a HashMap and LinkedTreeMap in C (05-01)
description:
In this series of lectures we implement a HashMap and a LinkedTreeMap beneath our Map abstraction.  The HashMap implementation builds on our ListMap implementation in the previous lectures.

For more details and a set of exercises please visit https://www.cc4e.com
For the full curriculum, see https://online.dr-chuck.com/

title: Understanding Hash Computations (05-02)
description:
Before we build our HashMap object implementation in C, we need to understand how the mathematics and theory of hashing works in general.  And in particular, we answer the world's most popular programming interview question, "Please draw a hash map."

For more details and a set of exercises please visit https://www.cc4e.com
For the full curriculum, see https://online.dr-chuck.com/

title: Building a HashMap object in C (CC-05-03)
description:
In this lecture we go through the implementation for our HashMap implementation.

For more details and a set of exercises please visit https://www.cc4e.com
For the full curriculum, see https://online.dr-chuck.com/

title: Using Binary Trees as Data Structures (05-04)
description:
In this lecture we begin to build out LinkedTreeMap beneath our Map abstraction.  We first look at how trees work and then look at the issues that arise when we try to build an iterator for a tree.  Then we look at building a data structure that is at the same time a Tree and a Linked List which will be our data structure for the LinkedTreeMap.

For more details and a set of exercises please visit https://www.cc4e.com
For the full curriculum, see https://online.dr-chuck.com/

title: Inserting Data Into a LinkedTreeMap in C (05-05)
description:
In this lecture we look at all the use cases of the put() method in the LinkedTreeMap.  We look at searching the tree, updating values, and inserting values, all while simultaneously maintaining a proper tree and sorted linked list.

For more details and a set of exercises please visit https://www.cc4e.com
For the full curriculum, see https://online.dr-chuck.com/

title: Counting Words in Python and in C using a LinkedHashMap (05-06)
description:
In this lecture, we look back at the first code example in www.py4e.com - counting words.   We then implement this same program using our newly developed LinkedTreeMap object in C.

For more details and a set of exercises please visit https://www.cc4e.com
For the full curriculum, see https://online.dr-chuck.com/
